title: Honor and Shame
summary: Ancient Mediterranean society was an honor-shame culture. A person's standing was determined not by private moral virtue but by their reputation in the community — their honor rating. Every interaction was an implicit challenge-riposte negotiation: someone attempted to claim honor at another's expense; the other was expected to respond in kind or concede status.
what_it_means_for_reading: When biblical characters act in ways that seem extreme or inexplicable, often they are defending or restoring honor. When Jesus is shamed (mocked, stripped, publicly executed), the resurrection is his vindication and honor-restoration. Paul's apparent shame (imprisonment, beatings, hardship) was a profound crisis for his converts — he devotes significant energy to reframing suffering as paradoxically honorable.

key_concepts.explanation: Social standing bestowed by the community. Ascribed honor came from birth and family; achieved honor from deeds recognized by peers.

Loss of social standing. Not identical to guilt — shame is about what others think, not private conscience. Cultures differ in whether shame is self-referential (Western) or other-referential (Mediterranean).
The social game of competitive honor-claim and response. Refusing to engage was itself a concession of status. Jesus's counter-questions to opponents are masterful challenge-riposte moves.
The system of social bonds between a superior (patron) who provides resources/protection and an inferior (client) who provides loyalty, gratitude, and public honor to the patron.

further_reading: Bruce Malina, The New Testament World; David deSilva, Honor, Patronage, Kinship & Purity

title: Patronage and Client Relations
summary: The ancient world ran on personal patron-client relationships rather than impersonal institutions. A patron (wealthy, powerful person) provided material support, legal protection, and social access to clients, who in return gave public honor, loyalty, and service. This was the primary social safety net — welfare state, legal system, and employment agency all rolled into one relationship.
what_it_means_for_reading: The entire biblical vocabulary of grace, mercy, benefaction, and obligation operates within patron-client logic. God is the ultimate Patron; Israel/believers are clients. Prayers are petitions to a patron. Praise and thanksgiving are the client's expected return of honor. Paul's language of 'grace' (χάρις) — a gift creating obligation — makes full sense only in this framework. The Lord's Supper is a patron's benefaction to his household.

key_concepts.explanation: The one who provides the gift. In Luke 22:25, Jesus rejects the Gentile style of 'benefactors who lord it over' — pointing to a different form of patronage: servant leadership.
The gift given by a patron that creates social obligation in the recipient. Not free — it binds the client to gratitude (εὐχαριστία) and public honor (δόξα) returned to the patron.
A go-between who connects clients to patrons they cannot access directly. Jesus is the broker who gives access to God the Father (John 14:6).

further_reading: Frederick Danker, Benefactor; deSilva, Honor, Patronage, Kinship & Purity

title: Purity and Pollution
summary: Ancient Israelite (and broader Mediterranean) society was organized around a purity system — a map of what belongs where. Certain persons, animals, conditions, and behaviors were 'pure' (belonging to God's order) or 'impure' (out of place in God's order). The system was not primarily about hygiene but about holiness — God's ordering of creation. Impurity was contagious and exclusionary.
what_it_means_for_reading: Jesus's ministry systematically crosses purity boundaries: he touches lepers, is touched by a bleeding woman, enters Gentile territory, eats with sinners. Each is a deliberate purity violation. But Jesus's pattern is not that impurity is contagious to him — his holiness is contagious to the impure (the leper is cleansed by his touch). This is a revolutionary inversion of the purity dynamic.

key_concepts.explanation: Belonging to God's proper order. Things, people, and times set apart for God's presence.
Out of place in God's ordered world. Not always sinful — a corpse, menstruation, and skin disease were unclean without implying moral fault. Impurity prevented approach to God's presence.
Impurity spreads by contact. Touching the impure transferred impurity to the person touching. Jesus's healings invert this: he touches the impure and holiness flows outward, not impurity inward.
The highest category of impurity — what is categorically incompatible with God's presence. Often refers to mixing categories (Lev 18–20).

further_reading: Mary Douglas, Purity and Danger; Jerome Neyrey, Purity, Power and Pollution in Mark's Gospel

title: Kinship and Household (οἶκος)
summary: In the ancient world, the household (oikos) was the basic social, economic, and religious unit — not the nuclear family or the individual. A household included the patriarch, wife, children, slaves, and dependent clients. The patriarch (paterfamilias) held near-absolute authority. Honor and shame were collective — what happened to one member affected the entire family's standing.
what_it_means_for_reading: When the NT speaks of 'household baptisms' (Acts 16:15, 33; 1 Cor 1:16), it means the entire household was baptized as a unit at the patriarch's conversion — not necessarily that each member had individual faith. The 'household codes' (Eph 5:22–6:9; Col 3:18–4:1) address household relationships in terms of ancient status-roles but transform them with mutual submission under Christ. The church as 'household of God' (1 Tim 3:15; Eph 2:19) uses this framework.

key_concepts.explanation: The male head of household who held authority over all members. His decision determined the religious affiliation and social standing of everyone under him.
Lists of duties for household members (husbands/wives, parents/children, masters/slaves). Found in Eph 5–6, Col 3–4, 1 Pet 2–3. Modified by mutual submission to Christ.
The early church as a new 'family' — using kinship language (brothers, sisters, fathers, mothers) to describe non-biological bonds that were just as binding as blood-family obligations.

further_reading: Carolyn Osiek & David Balch, Families in the New Testament World; Rodney Stark, The Rise of Christianity

title: Covenant (בְּרִית / διαθήκη)
summary: Covenant is the organizing framework of all Scripture. A covenant is not merely a contract — it is a binding relationship that creates family-like bonds between parties. Ancient Near Eastern covenants had a fixed form: preamble (who is making it), historical prologue (what I've done for you), stipulations (what you commit to), sanctions (blessings for faithfulness, curses for breach), and provisions for deposit and ratification.
what_it_means_for_reading: The Law at Sinai is covenant law — not a way to earn God's favor but the covenant stipulations for those already in the covenant relationship (Israel already redeemed from Egypt). The prophets' accusations against Israel are covenant lawsuits (rîb): you have breached the covenant. Jesus's 'new covenant in my blood' (Luke 22:20) is the fulfillment and transformation of the Mosaic covenant — not its abolition. Paul's 'covenant' theology in Galatians and Romans makes complete sense only against this ANE background.

key_concepts.explanation: The ANE template for Sinai covenant: the great king (YHWH) makes an agreement with a lesser king (Israel). The Hittite treaty form maps exactly onto Deuteronomy's structure.

Covenant loyalty/lovingkindness. The commitment to fulfill covenant obligations regardless of cost. Often translated 'steadfast love' or 'mercy' — better understood as covenant faithfulness under pressure.
The prophetic genre where YHWH summons heaven and earth as witnesses against Israel for covenant breach. Isa 1, Mic 6, Hos 4 all follow this legal form.

further_reading: Meredith Kline, The Treaty of the Great King; John Walton, Ancient Near Eastern Thought and the Old Testament

title: Temple, Presence, and Sacred Space
summary: In the ancient world, temples were not primarily places of assembly for worship — they were the dwelling-place of the god. The temple was the axis mundi (center/navel of the universe) where heaven and earth met. The innermost chamber (Holy of Holies) was where the god's actual presence resided. Access was hierarchically restricted — most people had no access to the inner sanctuary.
what_it_means_for_reading: The Israelite tabernacle/temple is organized as a graduated access system: Gentiles in the outer court, Israelites in the inner court, priests in the Holy Place, the High Priest alone in the Holy of Holies — once a year. The torn curtain at the crucifixion (Mark 15:38) is not primarily about Jesus 'opening a way in' to God — it is about God coming OUT. The presence of God is no longer restricted to the Holy of Holies but is now accessible to all through Christ. The church as 'temple of the Holy Spirit' (1 Cor 6:19; Eph 2:21) means God's presence now dwells in the community.

key_concepts.explanation: The visible manifestation of God's presence — the cloud and fire that filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34–38). When the glory departed (Ezek 10–11), it was the ultimate sign of judgment. Ezekiel 43's return of the glory is the great restoration hope.
The innermost chamber of the temple, housing the Ark of the Covenant, entered only by the High Priest on Yom Kippur. Separated from the Holy Place by the great veil (paroket).

The temple grounds organized space on a purity gradient: increasingly holy spaces moving inward, with increasingly restricted access. The Gentile court, women's court, Israel's court, priests' court — each more restricted.

further_reading: G.K. Beale, The Temple and the Church's Mission; Jon Levenson, Sinai and Zion

title: Sacrifice and Atonement
summary: Sacrifice was the central institution of ancient religion — not merely a Jewish peculiarity. In the ancient world, sacrifice was the primary means of communication with the divine: maintaining relationship, expressing dependence, dealing with pollution, and restoring broken order. Israelite sacrifice was distinctive in that it was directed to a personal God in covenant relationship with his people.
what_it_means_for_reading: The NT's use of sacrifice language for Jesus's death is not arbitrary metaphor — it is the fundamental category through which the NT interprets the crucifixion. Jesus is simultaneously the sacrifice (the offering) and the sacrificer (the priest). Hebrews develops this at length: Jesus is the true High Priest offering himself as the true sacrifice, fulfilling and rendering obsolete the entire Levitical system. The Lord's Supper is a participation in this sacrifice (1 Cor 10:16–17).

key_concepts.explanation: Wholly consumed offering expressing total dedication to God. No part returned to the worshiper — complete surrender. Background to Rom 12:1 ('living sacrifice').
Shared meal between God and worshiper — fat portions burned for God, meat eaten by priests and worshipers. The model for the Lord's Supper as fellowship meal.
Offering for inadvertent sin and defilement. Purified the sanctuary from pollution caused by sin. Distinguished from intentional ('high-handed') sin, which had no sacrifice.

Annual ritual in Lev 16: two goats — one killed to purify the sanctuary, one (Azazel, 'scapegoat') sent into the wilderness carrying the sins of the people. Hebrews 9–10 interprets Christ's death in terms of this ritual.

further_reading: Frank Gorman, Divine Presence and Community: A Commentary on Leviticus; Nicholas Kiuchi, Leviticus (Apollos OT Commentary)

title: Apocalyptic Worldview
summary: Apocalyptic literature is a genre that flourished from roughly 200 BCE to 200 CE. It presents revelation of heavenly/divine secrets through symbolic visions, often involving bizarre imagery (composite beasts, cosmic battles, heavenly journeys), a dualistic worldview (this age vs. the age to come, good spiritual powers vs. evil powers), and the expectation of a climactic divine intervention to end the current age and establish God's kingdom.
what_it_means_for_reading: Apocalyptic imagery is not meant to be decoded as a newspaper or a prediction of historical events in chronological order. The imagery is symbolic and functional — it communicates theological reality through pictorial language. When Revelation describes a beast with seven heads and ten horns, this is not a literal future monster — it is symbolic communication about the nature of empire, power, and opposition to God. The same beast-imagery in Daniel refers to historical empires (Babylon, Persia, Greece, Rome).

key_concepts.term: This Age / The Age to Come
key_concepts.explanation: The fundamental apocalyptic dualism: the current world order (dominated by sin and death) versus God's coming new order. The NT's radical claim is that the Age to Come has already broken in through Jesus's resurrection — but the Old Age has not yet ended.

The heavenly figure who receives universal dominion from the Ancient of Days. In Daniel, represents the faithful people of God; in Jesus's use, refers to himself as the one who embodies and leads the redeemed human community.
In Daniel 2 and 7, successive world empires represented by metals (statue) or beasts (sea creatures) — Babylon, Persia, Greece, Rome. The divine kingdom replaces them all.

Behind earthly political events, apocalyptic literature sees cosmic spiritual warfare. Daniel's 'Prince of Persia' (10:13), Paul's 'powers and principalities' (Eph 6:12), and Revelation's dragon are all instances of this framework.

further_reading: N.T. Wright, The New Testament and the People of God; John Collins, The Apocalyptic Imagination

title: Oral Culture and Memory
summary: The biblical world was predominantly oral — most people could not read, and transmission of tradition was primarily by memory and recitation rather than written texts. Oral cultures have distinct patterns of thought, composition, and transmission that are different from literate cultures. Repetition, formulaic phrases, mnemonic structures (chiasm, acrostic, refrain), and communal performance were the primary means of preserving tradition.
what_it_means_for_reading: Gospel 'discrepancies' in parallel accounts are not necessarily errors or contradictions — they often reflect the normal variation of oral performance tradition. The sayings of Jesus circulated as oral tradition before being written down; the Gospels crystallize particular performances. Repetition and variation are oral features, not literary flaws. The mnemonic structures (chiasm, parallelism, refrain) in both OT and NT poetry were memory aids — the form was part of the meaning.

key_concepts.explanation: Stories and sayings that circulate in a community by telling and re-telling, each performance adapting to the occasion while preserving the essential core. The Gospels represent stabilized performances.

In oral culture, eyewitness testimony is the primary form of historical evidence. The NT's appeal to eyewitnesses (Luke 1:2; 1 Cor 15:5–8; 1 John 1:1–3) reflects this culture of personal testimony as guarantor of truth.
The small elite of literate scribes (perhaps 3–5% of population) who produced, copied, and preserved written texts. They were the cultural gatekeepers of textual tradition.

further_reading: Kenneth Bailey, Paul and Peasant; Richard Bauckham, Jesus and the Eyewitnesses

title: Roman Imperial Context
summary: The NT was written under Roman imperial rule, which was not merely a political backdrop but a totalizing system of power, theology, and culture. The emperor was divine (or semi-divine), and the Roman peace (Pax Romana) was enforced by violence. Imperial propaganda saturated the culture: coins bore the emperor's image and titles ('Son of God,' 'Lord,' 'Savior of the World'), public spaces were filled with his statues and altars.
what_it_means_for_reading: The NT's use of 'gospel' (εὐαγγέλιον), 'Lord' (κύριος), 'Son of God' (υἱὸς θεοῦ), 'Savior' (σωτήρ), and 'peace' (εἰρήνη) are all loaded imperial terms — the same vocabulary used for Caesar. When Paul writes 'Jesus is Lord,' he is simultaneously confessing Jesus and confronting Caesar. Revelation's 'Babylon' is Rome. The imperial context shows the NT is not merely personal religion — it is a counter-imperial political theology.

key_concepts.explanation: The emperor was worshiped as divine or as the son of a divine father. Refusal to offer sacrifice to the emperor's genius was the primary source of Christian persecution.

The official 'arrival' of the emperor at a city — a formal visitation with procession, ceremony, and gifts. Paul uses this word for Christ's 'coming' — a counter-imperial arrival that outranks Caesar's.
The 'Roman Peace' — the internal stability of the empire maintained by military force. Paul's 'peace that surpasses understanding' (Phil 4:7) is an implicit critique of this peace-by-violence.

further_reading: N.T. Wright, Paul and the Faithfulness of God; Warren Carter, Matthew and the Margins

title: Second Temple Judaism
summary: The Judaism of Jesus's day was not uniform 'Old Testament religion' — it was a complex, diverse set of movements all responding to the same basic situation: Israel under foreign rule, awaiting restoration. Major groups included Pharisees (oral Torah, resurrection), Sadducees (temple, no resurrection), Essenes (sectarian purity, end-time expectation), and various apocalyptic/revolutionary movements. All shared certain convictions: monotheism, election, Torah, and the hope of national restoration.
what_it_means_for_reading: Many of Jesus's and Paul's teaching only makes sense against this Second Temple backdrop. 'The resurrection' in Acts 23:8 is already a contested doctrine between Pharisees and Sadducees — Paul's exploitation of this is a tactical masterstroke. Jesus's debates with the Pharisees are internal Jewish debates about interpretation, not Christianity vs. Judaism. The Dead Sea Scrolls (Qumran community, Essenes) illuminate many NT phrases and concepts that had no other parallel.

key_concepts.explanation: The Pharisaic oral Torah — 'the tradition of the elders' (Mark 7:3) — was an attempt to build a 'fence around the Torah' by specifying how it applies in every situation. Jesus's debates with them are about hermeneutical method, not rejection of Torah.
Diverse and often conflicting: a Davidic warrior-king who drives out Rome, a priestly Messiah who purifies the temple, a heavenly Son of Man figure. Jesus's messiahship confounded all of them.
The dominant prophetic hope: a new Exodus in which God would return to Zion, gather scattered Israel, restore the Davidic kingdom, and defeat the nations. Isaiah 40–55 was the primary template.

further_reading: E.P. Sanders, Paul and Palestinian Judaism; N.T. Wright, The New Testament and the People of God

title: Ancient Near Eastern Cosmology
summary: The biblical writers shared the cosmology of their ancient world: a three-tiered universe of heavens above, earth in the middle, and the underworld (Sheol) below. The sky was a solid dome (raqia — 'firmament') separating the waters above from the waters below. The earth was a flat disc surrounded by the cosmic sea. God's throne was in the highest heaven. This cosmology is the pictorial backdrop for much biblical imagery — it is not meant as scientific description.
what_it_means_for_reading: When Genesis 1 speaks of God separating the waters above from the waters below and placing the luminaries in the raqia — this is using the shared cosmological picture of the ancient world to make theological claims (God is the creator and orderer of all things), not providing a scientific account of the Big Bang. The imagery of 'the heavens opened' (Mark 1:10; Acts 7:56; Rev 4:1) is the ancient picture of the solid dome being parted to allow access to the divine realm. Interpreting these cosmological images literally confuses genre and purpose.

key_concepts.explanation: The solid dome of the sky, holding back the waters above. God placed the sun, moon, and stars in this dome. The flood involved opening 'the windows of heaven' (Gen 7:11) to let the waters above pour through.

The underworld, realm of the dead — a shadowy, undifferentiated place beneath the earth. Not identical to the NT's 'hell' (Gehenna) — the OT Sheol is where all the dead go, righteous and wicked alike.

The primordial chaotic deep (tehom/tiamat) that surrounds and underlies the earth. In the ancient world, the sea represented chaos, death, and the anti-creation forces. God's mastery over the sea is a recurring creation-theology motif.

further_reading: John Walton, The Lost World of Genesis One; Tremper Longman, How to Read Genesis
